replies.author: @whiskybrush3219
replies.text:
No, Fox decided to throw all their capital into the reality TV phenomenon which was only beginning to gain momentum at the time.  Firefly was an incredibly expensive show to produce whereas reality tv competitions could produce an entire "season" of 1 show for the cost of 1 episode of Firefly.   

The dispute with executives was over the order of airing. Whedon was pretty adamant that the show was produced and meant to be watched serially and execs had a different opinion on it, as up until then, tv shows were generally stand alone scenarios per episode with no particularly relevant precedent or follow up.  This decision proved to be pretty catastrophic as most viewers that tuned in for the premiere had zero context and didnt bother tuning in for the second episode, giving abysmal viewership numbers right out the gate.

Imagine still using “simp” unironically in 2025 and mocking people for living in their parents’ basements, in this economy. Wild.

I never lived at home myself, but I know Bill Gates, Steve Jobs, Michael Dell (Dell), Mark Zuckerberg, Evan Spiegel (Snapchat), Walt Disney, Daymond John (FUBU), and Richard Branson all did while launching their businesses. The Google co-founders started out in someone’s mom’s garage.

Meanwhile, today’s grads carry student debt that’s 1.4× higher than a decade ago, face housing costs up to 5× their income, and, despite having master’s degrees, often earn less in real wages than their parents did with just a high school diploma.

Honestly, I admire the people who are thugging it out, working their asses off to build something better, even if that means living in their parents’ garage. That takes a lot more grit than racking up debt to drive a car you can’t afford.

Say what you want about Taika, people like what they like. But this habit of mocking others to feel superior? Honestly, that’s more “simp” than anything the so-called simps are doing.

replies.text:
​@phoenixdzk what are you even talking about? Even your own examples don't support that. 

I'd somewhat grant that the Krypto was *_arguably_* used for sympathy points. Though even then it definitely had greater significance, with Superman feeling obliged to save him despite him 'not even being a good dog which, in a better movie, would've played into a larger arc of characterizing this version of Superman more thoroughly. 

I could even tentatively grant that the 'spider rabbit' was as well (though most of their scenes are actually representing a safe-space for Rocket where we don't, generally, see anything bad happen. Most of the horror/sympathy comes from the fact that they exist and were only made as prototypes for a world they'd never get to see or be a part of, but the actual scenes they're in are all generally more lighthearted & earnest periods of safety & relief for Rocket. (And this is ignoring the more obvious issue of whether you can even call them 'animals' in this sense. They're sapeint beings who act just like humans that hold emotional weight because of how they've been characterized, not just because "they're animals". If anything their appearance superficially makes them more horrifying and unnatural, which directly works *_against_* the point you're making.)

But when the fuck was Peacemaker's eagle used for sympathy points? I have *_more_* than my share of problems with that show (dear god that one black woman was just utterly insufferable. I mean none of them were great but she was just so clearly a mouthpiece and basically nothing else. I remember basically nothing about her other than her being annoying and her one absolutely infuriating monologue at the end of the show where she said Peacemaker's libertarianism is proto-fascist or whatever, even though those are literally complete opposite sides of the political spectrum.) but I cannot remember a single scene where the Eagle was even somewhat put in danger or used for sympathy points.

Also, in The Suicide Squad one of the most talked about scenes is the one with Bloodspot and Rat Catcher on the train, literally just earnestly trauma dumping on eachother. Yes, those traumas *_involved_* animals but, again, if anything this works *_against_* your point since Bloodpsort being thrown into a box of rats makes the animals the point of aggression, not the ones suffering. (plus, in-general most people really don't care about rats as a default. You can argue whether it's right or not but it's true. Rats are seen as a pest, just one step up from something like a cockroach. Hell that's literally the reason Rat Cathcer II gave for *_why_* her dad invented the rat controlling technology; "Rats are the lowliest and most despised of all creatures, my love. replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
He recombined things. So buffy is teen drama with horror and kickboxing. Firefly was western merged with sci fi, topped with southern rebel imagery that I'd assume he got from the dukes of Hazard. Alien 4 was him putting the previous Alien films into a blender. Throwing together unexpected elements gives you novelty value, but it still seemed like a gimmick. His methodology might have worn thin if he was still putting women based on Sarah Connor into things. At some point, people would have thought 'I've seen this before'. He probably would have needed to reinvent himself. Otherwise, he'd wind up as Tim Burton repeating himself 30 years later. He might not have had anything new to say and might have been trapped by diminishing returns. People don't usually maintain their spot in the cultural zeitgeist indefinitely. They burn out or the culture moves to a new place. And I'd wonder if his rise and the thought of putting genres in a blender for the sake of novelty led to 'Pride and Prejudice vs Zombies' and 'Abraham Lincoln, Vampire Hunter'.

Most great directors had limited windows and then repeated themselves or had lost their revolutionary edge. Same with rock bands. You explode out of the gate and get a couple albums before people work you out. 'The Nevers' sounded like more of the same.

text: I'm glad the Critical Drinker pointed out that it isn't Whedon's style of writing that is flawed but the cheap imitations of it that we have been subjected to. Humor isn't easy to write, so this should not be a surprise. It takes real talent to do what Whedon did, something that most Hollywood writers today are sorely lacking. Regarding the failure of Justice League, I don't think it's fair to blame Whedon. He simply did what the Warner executives told him to do. He was told to cut the film down to two hours and to add humor. It didn't work, but it was an incredibly difficult task. The Warner execs should have left the film as is. Panic moves rarely work, and this was no exception.

​@donello430 Non-binary seems to be more about *reinforcing* gender stereotypes than rejecting them. 

The thought process goes something like: "Guys are supposed to act a certain way. If I don't act like a stereotypical guy, then ... I must not be a guy! I should label myself non-binary!" 

By insisting that they don't belong in the blue room, they're confirming that they believe the blue room exists, and any man who feels uncomfortable in that room is not actually a man and should go find another room. Likewise with women uncomfortable in the pink room. 

Someone who truly rejected gender stereotypes would say, "I'm obviously a male because biology, but I'll act however I please and like whatever I like, thank you very much. Let's repaint these silly blue and pink rooms in a whole range of colours, and knock down the walls between them."

Yep.  I go back to when we had that strike way back when... when Whedon called up all his friends and asked them if they wanted to make something, but there'd be no contracts, no money, nada, because it would be a breach in the strike if they did.

They unanimously said YES.

And that was how Doctor Horrible was made, during a strike, with people who simply loved working with Joss.  Yet, today, we have all these people coming out against him?  Something happened that motivated them to tell lies or exaggerate.  He crossed some line with the public trends and political agendas and narratives. author: @Tiah-mm8lp
text:
I normally agree with you, but those first 3 seasons of Buffy are some of the greatest storytelling on TV in a time when we were waiting on a wish for superheroes to get a chance by Hollywood. (Now we take it for granted, we're so spoiled with superhero stories) Angel the spinoff, was all great, except for the Connor episodes. And his Avenger films were incredible. It makes sense why he was hired.

When I revisited them (the first 3 seasons) as an adult I discovered stuff that I hadn't really noticed like: 
-Defeating the Master was her saying goodbye to her youth
-She wasn't really fighting demons, but how nasty/awful kids can be to one another in high school 
-The first boyfriend is the definition of what a boyfriend is supposed to be, he's there for her and cares for her wellbeing until she gives him what he wants, and all of a sudden he walks all over her and turns into something else. 

All that stuff, seeing it as an adult, I never got it as a kid, it gave me a new found respect. The thought that went into this, it was brilliant writing.

Sure, episodes would grant her time to fix stuff because fortunately there were other problems going on as well.

Here's a summary of her character...

"In the "Firefly" universe, Kaylee Frye is known for her exceptional intuitive understanding of machines, especially her role as the Serenity's mechanic. Despite lacking formal engineering training, she possesses a natural talent for fixing and understanding complex machinery. This innate ability allows her to diagnose and repair issues with the ship that even Simon Tam, a trained medic, initially struggles with. Her intuition is a key part of her character and is often highlighted in the show."

Tada. "Intuition, natural, innate ability." There's NEVER any explanation of how she actually learned stuff with experience (i.e. informal training) even though it should have been mentioned. You made that up for yourself in your head. It always stood out for me when watching the show, it's just weak writing that didn't damage a very enjoyable show too much. As much as folks liked the actor, Scotty character was never the strong part of Trek either.

replies.author: @TrykusMykus
replies.text:
@TheBloopers30 I mean that's fair. I understand why people like the movie (a chance to see the characters again), but to me, there were too many inconsistencies between the movie and the TV characters. Also between the story. The TV story was something I would call a low sci-fi (like low fantasy, stories of ordinary people where their lives change, but the world doesn't.), the movie was high sci-fi instead (like most fantasy, you have LOTR, GOT and all other series where you follow kings taking down other kings to change the world).

Also, there were overpowered characters for as long as they needed to be overpowered. If the assassin kept the power he had in any other scene when he met Mal, he would have murdered him and Inara instantly after getting shot. River is overpowered and showing it only when the plot needs her to. Reavers were overpowered until they needed to be mowed down by 6 people out of whom 2 could actually be considered soldiers.

Kaylee felt like a completely different person. Skipping past the sex hunger, why was she shooting the reavers? Last time she had a gun, she couldn't fire it - to protect herself or the people she cared about. Now she's shooting everyone?

The story just felt lazy. It needed to progress so something progressed it, no matter if it made sense or not. The questions I had after the series were not answered properly either (Inara saying stuff like "Which Mal am I getting today?" when she left after he protected an establishment FOR HER). Maybe I just expected something other than what I got.

​@GodwynDi It depends on why you're there, if the boss holds themselves to the same standards, and if they reward you for meeting the high standards they set or just raise them and give you more abuse. When I was younger, I had one boss that was just a bully. She'd intentionally give people orders to do the wrong thing, then pretend they were idiots that couldn't do their job while flipping out on them in front of everyone. She literally laughed and made jokes the next day about reducing a teenage girl to tears in front of customers. I'm not a fan of bullies, so I delighted in messing with her right up until my last day.

On the other hand, I had a job in college where my boss was just a complete prick. He singled me out and treated me like garbage trying to get me to quit. Eventually I realized I was actually the problem, I was lazy, barely competent, and just collecting a paycheck until I finished school and got a "real" job. I turned it around pretty quick, ended up being one of the few people trusted to do all the training for the new people, and worked there for years afterwords. I ended up really enjoying the job and making a lot of good friends there. I won't say I ever became friendly with that boss, he was always a bit of a prick on his best days, but I learned a lot, and my work ethic wouldn't be nearly as good without that experience.

replies.text:
​@BC-ny3zb your mistake there is the word 'another' adult. If you think you're beyond being yelled at, you're not an adult, your a child who's prestiged. An *_actual_* adult would *_demand_* they be called out for when they're underperforming, so that they can correct it. That is how things are supposed to work, if there is an issue, you say it, and it gets corrected. You should *_want_* people you work with to be willing to tell you when you're fucking up so that you can actually fix the issue and, ideally yes, the inverse should be true so that when you *_do_* perform well it's rewarded.

If people refuse to either say the issue or correct the issue, things only get worse until either one of those two things change *_or_* someone involved is fired and things completely fall apart.

An adult understands that they are fallible and not beyond scolding if they fuck up. That is *_why_* the trope of the yelling drill seargent exists; in something like the military when it is literally a matter of life and death people need to get yelled at to understand "you are not special, shape the fuck up". (that's *_also_* why "The Old Bastards" 77th infantry division were so wildly competent; these were people who already *_learned_* that lesson from living life for an extra decade, so they policed *_themselves_* and kept *_themselves_* on track.)

author: @flatline8199
text:
It should be pointed out that, when actually looked at, the things that were corroborated, Whedon's "abuse" basically boils down to shouting at people and sarcasm. Given directors have - without much complaint - nearly killed people in the past to get shots, with nor much fanfare about it, this seems pretty tame. If this had come out in the 2010s, nobody would have cared much. The "abuse" was - as far as I can tell - blown out of all proportion by the height of the over-sensitivity era.

As an aside, re: Charisma Carpenter, it should be pointed out that they worked together in two long-running series. If he didn't like her, he wouldn't have hired her for a second show, and if she really he he was that abusive, would she have hired on for a second show, or worked with the man for a decade? I have my doubts. This is just a bunch of people trying to cash in drama to ignite or reignite their careers. Justice League notwithstanding.

Both Fillian and Marsters have openly said they never experienced or saw any of this so-called "abuse".

Edit: Tudyk has now said openly on a podcast that he saw and experienced nothing like this, nor did anybody on Firefly. Same time as Angel and Buffy, same man.

author: @jeffmcarthur5617
text: It is no coincidence that Whedon got called out right after a flop.  Notice how everyone has said he treated people horribly for decades, yet none of that came out when the movies and shows were successful.  That's because people in the industry, in particular producers, agents, and studio execs, were forcing people who were affected to be quiet.  Their profits were more important than their pain.  Even after Firefly and his next show failed, they knew they could make more money off of Whedon, so they repressed the information.  But after Justice League, it was clear they couldn't milk any more money off of him, so the actors were allowed to speak up.  Don't fool yourself; most of the people responsible for letting this shit happen are never punished.  They go on threatening and intimidating actors and crew if they don't let the ones making them money do whatever they want.  And these same producers, agents, and studio execs are often lauded as being the good ones because they signal a lot of fake virtues to gullible crowds.

author: @jaedencew
text:
Most of the cast of Buffy loved working with Whedon. Charisma Carpenter and Joss clashed over her becoming pregnant, and I think, not telling him until it was too late to write into the story. Other members of Buffy have come forward and said there was no rule that they were aware of that Joss couldn't be alone with Michelle Trachenburg.  Sarah Michelle Geller only said "I'd like my career not be just defined by what I did with Joss Whedon." Marsters, Boreanz,  Anthony Head have all been baffled by the charges and said they were never aware of any tension on set. Alysson Hannigan has never said anything that I'm aware of.
On Firefly, I've never heard a single cast member say anything but glowing reviews of filming, saying, years later, every single day was a joy and everyone loved the set and crew and they wanted it to last forever.
Whedon knew how to make a very dramatic scene and then a single line of dialogue that broke the tension and got a laugh. Modern writers have adapted that to mean that no dramatic scene ever happens, and every 30 seconds there has to be a joke. And that interpretation of what makes a good Superhero movie has essentially ruined them.
Looking at you James Gunn.

author: @Muxxyy
text:
As someone who worked on films for nearly a decade and quit last year - I have a lot of sympathy for Whedon. Hollywood is distinctly lacking in balls these days. Artistic clashes used to be accepted as a necessary evil - some of the best things on screen were created by people who openly hated each other. Those clashes came about because people passionately cared about their craft. But nowadays everyone prioritises emotional safety. Everyone is walking on glass for the fear of offending each other and personal relationships are prioritised over the quality of the product. If you wondered why movies are so terrible these days - that's the reason. People on set don't care about the movie, they care about "networking" and "building relationships ". It's not a job, it's daycare for kidults. It's a dead industry walking, and I'm glad I got out.

Edit: to all the blind people who are commenting- I'm a woman. I was lucky enough to meet some wonderful and very genuine guys early in my career who remained mentors to this day. But all the good guys are retired now, and industry is flooded with fake talentless arse-kissers. It's just so shallow and uninspiring, so I've moved onto greener pastures.

These days, Producers keep Directors on a tight leash and if they go over budget there are all kinds of penalties written into their contracts.

I can absolutely understand a Director who loses his temper on a set when actors or crew are goofing off and holding up the production. Nobody ever goes to " threatening someone's career" as a first option- it's always the last resort. But I have seen it happen, it was always justified.

If you're misbehaving on set - you're threatening the livelihoods of everyone involved in the production. It's a zero-tolerance zone and the Director has a power to make you behave in whatever way may seem most suitable at that moment.

author: @shawnsmith2006
text:
I loved the Buffy movie so much that I didn't even watch the show until a couple of seasons into it, because of the cast being different. I eventually accepted that that is usually how tv shows based on movies do it, though, plus i realized it was a continuation of the film and not a reboot. 

I still think the film was more epic, and had one of the best soundtracks from a movie. The only issue I had with the film is right before the end, before she kills  Dracula, I think the writing could have been tweaked a little. I didn't really understand that part too much when I first watched it. The very end where she rides off with her boyfriend, after the prom night bloodbath, at early light with the credits rolling, with "we close our eyes", playing, was a pretty great ending. That was another reason I didn't really want a show because I liked the ending of the film and thought it was pretty self contained, but an equally good sequel, I would have been up for. 

I finally started watching the series when I saw clips with David Boreanaz in it. I thought the show was pretty good and also liked Angel. I probably was never a die hard fan of them like some people, but I thought they were mostly good. I still like the film better and it just felt more like what the Buffy character was supposed to be and had the right atmosphere, even with it just being a 90 minute film. It's one of those films, I can watch a million times. The show was a little mopey to me at times and I didn't always like thr 'scooby gang' aspect of the show, or how they made Buffy's mom more sympathetic on the show then she was in the film, but I guess kept the father being self centered, I think, I don't know if I'm remembering her father correctly on the show. That was some early woke stuff there.

author: @misterfinnster3562
text: I once thought that Joss Whedon was the best writer/director in Hollywood. I worked in the entertainment industry, and I worked with someone who was heavily involved with Buffy the Vampire Slayer during its first four seasons. I wad excited to hear the behind-the-scenes stories. I asked him what Joss Whedon was like. He stopped, thought a moment, and said “Joss is an absolute genius, but he is the worst human being I have ever known.” This was years before everything was exposed. He told me a lot of things that Whedon did, and some of them were difficult to stomach. I won’t diminish Joss’ work, because the majority of it is exceptional, but he got what he deserved. I think he could make a comeback, but it would require him to be humble, accept complete responsibility for his actions, and he sincerely apologetic to the people he wronged.

author: @highlander31527
text:
Controversial opinion time.
First, I am not going to compare the accomplishments of the two director/writers, or their subject matter, but merely draw a parallel, and a common point.
Joss Whedon and Stanley Kubrick. There is a reason for this.
Both were perfectionists. Both wanted the best performance from every actor, all of the time in their storytelling, no matter how many times a scene had to be reshot or redone. Both worked endlessly to get the story the way they wanted it told, and both have their unique styles, but both demanded the actors in their works give the most effort whether it was the tenth shoot or the first.
Now, for the connection.
Adam Baldwin has worked with both directors. I recall a story about Adam talking about having to reshoot a scene during Full Metal Jacket. Adam was talking with another castmate, griping about it being the tenth time to do the scene or something and Kubrick overheard him and called him out for a talking to. Whatever was said made enough of an impression that Adam didn't gripe anymore. 
That being said, Adam later worked with Joss on more than one project, and never had a bad word to say about Joss. Adam was written into Angel as a secondary or tertiary antagonist in the fifth season for absolutely no reason, and owned the role of the Senior Partners as their hand on Earth. His portrayal of Hamilton was a positive in the story, but didn't have to be added. We already had Eve fulfilling that role. Nathan Fillion worked with Joss a lot also, and mentioned that Joss had high standards and that those standards had to be met.
It is clear that both directors are driven in their vision of what they want to give to people on screen, and are less than sugar and politeness when they are getting less than they expect from their actors. Joss had to do an entire rewrite on S4 of Angel when Charisma broke her contract and got pregnant and lied about it to Joss. When it became apparent during shooting, Joss lost it. Jewel Staite mentioned also that Joss was brilliant, but could be difficult to work with. You could also look at Eliza Dushku, who worked on multiple projects and has nothing but praise for Joss.
The short version of all of this is that both directors demand a high level of performance and if you don't give them your best, they will let you know. And if you actively sabotage them, they will let you know directly.
Some actors can take criticism, and some can't. Some directors can deal with actors phoning in a performance, and others take it personally when you don't try. I personally feel that Whedon has a sense of humor and does allow for some improvisation in his actors when they are on screen, as long as it helps the story. Like RDJ and his snack obsession and allowing him to alter some lines to fit Tony, or Jayne always needing to have something in his hands to be messing with while on screen.
Let us not forget also that 2020 was at the height of cancel culture, and anyone that had ever felt slighted decided that it was then time to destroy anyone involved in the matter.

author: @TheNoonish
text:
I honestly think there's a degree to which accusations against Whedon are blown out of proportion.  I think he's probably an asshole and a very difficult, demanding boss.  But that's the nugget of truth that adds credibility to the more extreme accusations.  For example, I don't buy that he was particularly racist against Ray Fisher-he was a hardass on all of the cast of Justice League, and even though he cut Fisher's stuff, he was stuck with a hard time cap imposed by the studio.  Seeing Zack Snyder's cut, it's embarrassing how little Superman there was, so it makes sense why he needed to reshoot stuff to bring Superman back into a story where Superman was resurrected from the dead.  (and also, it's the Justice League movie and he's freaking Superman)

I also can imagine he did take pride in making staffers cry if they did a bad job-but not women in particular.  Women are just more likely to be the ones who respond to harsh criticism by crying, and he was probably a hardass on every staff writer because he had demanding standards.  I don't doubt that he said and did a lot of nasty things to Charisma Carpenter, either.

Drinker actually doesn't disagree with you, and I went back to listen to listen to that timestamp you posted just to make sure. He's done a separate video about Firefly where he says he finally watched it years later and was blown away by how good it was, and goes on to explain why it wasn't given its fair shot. He's just summarizing those opinions in this video. He doesn't say anything to contradict your opinion about Firefly being Whedon's best work; all he does is briefly explain why it wasn't more visible and popular.

text: Very good take.  People seem to think there was sexual impropriety, but it seems he was just an ass and a bully.   He is a genius and an artist and a director.  A-hole is probably the default. Charisma Carpenter also complains about how she was treated on the set of Angel, but I think her main problem with him was the terrible story line he wrote for her character when she got pregnant.  I have heard it said that she didn't tell anyone until she was showing, and it messed up the story and really pissed him off, so he turned her into a demon having a demon baby, then put her in a coma until the last season. @1pcfred - I never saw it in first-run.  A client of mine told me about it.  He loved it.  When I was able to watch it, I guess I saw it in the order in which it was intended to be seen.  I was happy to see Ron Glass, who I hadn't seen since Barney Miller.  Ron Glass played his role SO well.  All the actors in the series were excellent.

I mean, you watch it and you say to yourself, "How the hell did this series not make it?"

replies.author: @GizmoFromPizmo
replies.text:
@joebeast15 - Man, think about Star Trek.  It was sold as a Western in space.  And, in truth, it did have certain of those qualities.  But Firefly was an ACTUAL Western in space.

They had pistols with bullets and not ray guns.  There was an old world quality that reminds you of Star Wars, in a way, but with more depth.

Like I said in my OP, the world building was just excellent.

I mean, the Serenity was carting around both a prostitute and a Christian minister and the show just worked.  The more I think about it, the more brilliant I'm convinced it was.
